draft.introduction: Wang Yangming’s Instructions for Practical Living articulates his doctrine of the unity of knowledge and action (zhi xing he yi), arguing that true knowledge is inseparable from moral practice. It represents the most radical turn in Neo-Confucian thought.

translation.original_ref: Wang Yangming, Chuan Xi Lu, Part 1
translation.text: I said: ‘Knowledge is the beginning of action; action is the completion of knowledge. When one truly knows, one acts. To know and not to act is simply not yet to know. Knowledge and action are one and the same. The innate knowledge of the good (liang zhi) is present in every person.’
translation.original_text: 知是行的主意，行是知的功夫

translator_notes.note: Wang Yangming’s doctrine that knowledge without action is not true knowledge challenges the scholarly culture of mere book-learning. His concept of liang zhi (innate moral knowledge) parallels Mencius’s view of inherent goodness and anticipates aspects of Western moral intuitionism.
